question: QED renormalisation involves three independent counterterms in the standard scheme. What are they?

options: $Z_2$ (electron field), $Z_3$ (photon field), $Z_m$ (mass); the vertex $Z_1$ equals $Z_2$ by Ward identity, so only 3 independent — same as the number of parameters in the bare Lagrangian
$Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_m$ — four independent counterterms required in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Only $Z_m$ is needed; field renormalisations are gauge-invariant in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
QED is finite, so no counterterms are required at any loop order in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: Bare Lagrangian (in terms of bare quantities): $\mathcal L_0 = \bar\psi_0(i\not\partial - m_0)\psi_0 - e_0\bar\psi_0\gamma^\mu\psi_0 A_{0\mu} - \tfrac14 F_0^2$. Rescale: $\psi_0 = \sqrt{Z_2}\psi_R$, $A_0 = \sqrt{Z_3}A_R$, $m_0 = Z_m m_R/Z_2$, $e_0 = e_R Z_1/(Z_2\sqrt Z_3)$. Ward: $Z_1 = Z_2$ → $e_0 = e_R/\sqrt Z_3$. Three independent constants for three Lagrangian parameters ($m, e, Z_\psi$).

pairs.definition: On-shell scheme: $\delta m = $ shift between bare and physical mass; logarithmic in cutoff: $\delta m \sim \alpha m\ln(\Lambda/m)$
Residue of full propagator at physical pole; gauge-dependent; appears in LSZ as $\sqrt Z_2$ per external electron leg
Gauge-invariant; determines running of $\alpha$ via $\alpha_R = \alpha_0 Z_3$; one-loop: $Z_3 = 1 + \alpha/(3\pi\epsilon) + \ldots$
Add $\delta\mathcal L = (Z-1)\mathcal L$ pieces to cancel divergences; choose finite parts (e.g., on-shell, $\overline{\rm MS}$, momentum-subtraction)

active.explanation: Renormalisation makes finite predictions from divergent loops. Different schemes give different finite values of intermediate quantities but same physical observables. On-shell scheme is intuitive (counterterms eliminate physical mass/charge shifts); $\overline{\rm MS}$ is convenient (just subtract $1/\epsilon$ pole).

question: The Ward identity $Z_1 = Z_2$ has a deep consequence for QED. What is it?

options: Universality of charge renormalisation: charge running depends only on photon self-energy ($Z_3$), not on which fermion (electron, muon, quark) is in the loop — all fermions of same charge run identically
$Z_1 = Z_2$ implies QED is non-renormalisable regardless of the choice of regularisation scheme used in the calculation
Identity holds only at one loop; breaks down at higher orders for any spacetime dimension above two without further modification needed
It allows the photon to acquire a mass via loop corrections for any spacetime dimension above two without further modification needed
passive.explanation: Bare coupling: $e_0 = e_R Z_1/(Z_2\sqrt Z_3) = e_R/\sqrt Z_3$ (using $Z_1 = Z_2$). Renormalised coupling depends only on $Z_3$, which is determined by photon self-energy. Universality: all charged particles see the same running $\alpha(\mu^2)$, regardless of their type. Consequence: precision QED tests (Lamb shift, $g - 2$, hydrogen spectrum, atomic clocks) all give consistent value of $\alpha$. Non-Abelian theories: Ward identity becomes Slavnov-Taylor; $Z_1 \neq Z_2$ in general.

question: The on-shell renormalisation scheme defines the electron mass and charge by physical conditions. What are they?

options: Electron mass = pole position of propagator ($\not p = m_R$); residue = $Z_2$; charge = Thomson scattering limit ($k^2 \to 0$ photon-electron coupling)
Mass and charge are taken at arbitrary off-shell momentum; no physical definition
On-shell scheme requires the cutoff $\Lambda$ to be specified
All renormalisation conditions are imposed at the Z-pole ($\sqrt s = 91$ GeV)
passive.explanation: On-shell (OS) scheme: define $m_R$ and $e_R$ by their physical, IR conditions. $m_R$ = pole of two-point function; $e_R$ = charge measured at low momentum (Thomson). Counterterms determined by these conditions; finite parts absorbed automatically. Convenient for direct comparison with measurements. Alternative: $\overline{\rm MS}$ (minimal subtraction with $\ln 4\pi - \gamma_E$ removal) — convenient for high-energy QFT; intermediate; momentum-subtraction (MOM) — defines couplings at some Euclidean reference momentum.

question: The running of $\alpha$ from low to high energy is well-measured. What's the experimental confirmation?

options: $1/\alpha(0) = 137.036$ (low $E$, Thomson limit); $1/\alpha(m_Z^2) = 127.94 \pm 0.02$ (measured at LEP/SLC); the $\sim 7\%$ shift directly tests vacuum polarisation
$\alpha$ has been measured constant from low to high energy; no running observed
Running of $\alpha$ is too small to measure experimentally
The running depends on choice of renormalisation scheme; no scheme-independent prediction
passive.explanation: Precision measurements: $\alpha$ at $\mu = 0$ from quantum Hall effect, atom interferometry, electron $g-2$. $\alpha(m_Z^2)$ from precision electroweak observables (effective leptonic mixing angle $\sin^2\theta_W^{\rm eff}$, $Z$ partial widths). Hadronic vacuum polarisation contribution dominates uncertainty in $\alpha(m_Z^2)$: $\Delta\alpha_{\rm had}(m_Z^2) \approx 0.0276 \pm 0.0001$. Sub-percent agreement: definitive confirmation of QED running.

question: BPHZ (Bogoliubov-Parasiuk-Hepp-Zimmermann) is the systematic recipe for QED renormalisation at all orders. What's its content?

options: Recursive subtraction of subdivergences: forest formula reorganises overlapping divergences; ensures locality of counterterms; proves renormalisability to all orders for renormalisable QFTs
BPHZ applies only to QED at one loop; higher orders require new techniques in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Recipe gives finite results without need for any counterterms in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
BPHZ regularisation explicitly breaks gauge invariance and so does not work for QED in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: BPHZ scheme (Bogoliubov-Parasiuk 1957, Hepp 1966, Zimmermann 1969): for each divergent subdiagram, subtract its Taylor expansion around external momentum zero. Forest formula handles nested/overlapping divergences. Proves all UV divergences in renormalisable QFT can be removed order by order by local counterterms. Modern viewpoint: subsumed by Wilson's RG approach. In practice: most QED computations use dim reg + $\overline{\rm MS}$ or on-shell scheme.

question: The renormalisation-group equation in QED gives the scale-evolution of physical observables. What's it for a scattering amplitude $\Gamma^{(n)}$?

options: $[\mu\partial_\mu + \beta(e)\partial_e - n_e\gamma_\psi + n_\gamma\gamma_A]\Gamma^{(n)} = 0$; running coupling + anomalous dimensions of external legs determine scale dependence
Amplitude is independent of renormalisation scale by construction in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
RG equation involves only the $\beta$-function, no anomalous dimensions in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Gives identical answers in all renormalisation schemes in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
passive.explanation: Anomalous dimension $\gamma_\psi = \tfrac12\mu d\ln Z_2/d\mu$ for electron field; similar $\gamma_A$ for photon. The 'minus' sign for $\gamma_\psi$ reflects that external-leg renormalisation contributes inversely. Solving RG equation with running coupling $e(\mu)$ resums large logs $\ln(E/m_e)$ in scattering amplitudes. Critical for processes at $E \gg m_e$ where leading-log accuracy demands RG improvement.

question: QED is believed to have a Landau pole at extreme high energy. What does this mean and is it physical?

options: $\alpha(\mu)$ formally diverges at $\mu_L \sim m_e e^{3\pi/2\alpha} \sim 10^{286}$ GeV via one-loop running; well above Planck scale, so QED is embedded in a larger UV-complete theory before reaching $\mu_L$
Landau pole is at $\mu_L = m_e c^2$ — i.e., at the electron mass itself in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
QED has no Landau pole; $\alpha$ runs only logarithmically forever in the standard QED textbook treatment, as established for perturbative regimes
Landau pole is physical and observable at LHC energies; the renormalisation-group flow leaves this conclusion unchanged at all orders
passive.explanation: Solving one-loop RG: $1/\alpha(\mu^2) = 1/\alpha(\mu_0^2) - (2/3\pi)\ln(\mu^2/\mu_0^2)$. Pole when $\alpha \to \infty$: $\mu_L \sim m_e\exp(3\pi/2\alpha_0)$ — astronomically above Planck. Higher loops modify but don't remove. Physical interpretation: QED is an effective theory embedded in some UV completion (GUT, string theory) well before this scale. In the SM, electroweak unification at $m_Z$ scale already extends QED; the U(1)_Y hypercharge has its own Landau pole at much smaller scale ($\sim 10^{53}$ GeV) — still above Planck.
